tracks.title: Start Here: CoT to Self-Consistency
tracks.hook: A compact route for understanding why reasoning traces became a first-class interface for LLMs.
tracks.audience: Students and engineers who want the shortest path from prompting to modern reasoning terminology.
tracks.why_hot: Almost every recent reasoning paper still uses CoT, self-consistency, and decomposition as its baseline vocabulary.
compare: Whether reasoning is produced only at inference time or also used for training.
Whether the method improves reasoning by better prompts, better sampling, or better data.
Whether rationales are treated as explanations, latent computation, or supervision signals.

tracks.title: Deliberate Search: Trees, Graphs, and Test-Time Compute
tracks.hook: A route through explicit search over thoughts and the newer idea that inference-time compute is itself a scaling axis.
tracks.audience: Readers interested in budget forcing, inference-time search, and non-greedy reasoning.
tracks.why_hot: Test-time scaling is one of the clearest bridges between old reasoning prompts and current reasoning models.
compare: How the search state is represented: chain, tree, graph, or budget-controlled continuation.
Whether additional compute is spent on sampling, search, verification, or longer thinking.
How much performance comes from inference strategy versus training data.

tracks.title: Agents, Tools, and Executable Reasoning
tracks.hook: A route through reasoning systems that leave pure text and interact with tools, code, memory, and environments.
tracks.audience: Agent builders, tool-use researchers, and people interested in executable reasoning traces.
tracks.why_hot: Tool-use and agent workflows are where reasoning becomes visible as actions instead of only text.
compare: Whether tools are called through prompting, fine-tuning, or self-supervised API annotations.
Whether the system learns from environment feedback, self-feedback, or external execution.
Whether the reasoning trace is natural language, code, or action-observation memory.

tracks.title: Verifiers, Process Rewards, and Self-Judging Models
tracks.hook: A route for understanding why final-answer accuracy is not enough and why step-level reward became central.
tracks.audience: Readers interested in PRMs, reward modeling, math verification, and scalable supervision.
tracks.why_hot: Reasoning RL needs reward signals; process supervision is one of the main ways to make those rewards less sparse.
compare: Outcome supervision versus process supervision.
Human annotations versus synthetic or model-generated step labels.
External verifiers versus self-judging models.

tracks.title: Reasoning RL Systems: GRPO, Long-CoT, and Open Reasoners
tracks.hook: The route that connects DeepSeekMath, DeepSeek-R1, Kimi k1.5, DAPO, ORZ, and Skywork-OR1.
tracks.audience: LLM researchers tracking modern RL recipes for math, code, and long-chain reasoning.
tracks.why_hot: This is the current high-traffic cluster: reasoning ability as an RL-trained behavior rather than only a prompt trick.
compare: Which parts are SFT, RL, rejection sampling, distillation, or verifier-driven selection.
How each system defines rewards for math/code correctness.
How much of the recipe depends on base model quality versus RL infrastructure.

tracks.title: Reasoning Data Recipes: Small Data, Open Data, and SFT/RL Synergy
tracks.hook: A route for readers who care less about slogans and more about where the reasoning behavior actually comes from.
tracks.audience: Students, reproduction builders, and open-source practitioners trying to assemble training data or starter pipelines.
tracks.why_hot: After DeepSeek-R1, many readers want recipes, not just model names: data filtering, distillation, SFT, and RL are the buy-in points.
compare: Tiny curated data versus large open reasoning corpora.
SFT-only unlocks versus RL-driven improvements.
Math-only recipes versus math-plus-code recipes.

tracks.title: Benchmarks and Stress Tests: Code, Browsing, Expert Exams, and Robustness
tracks.hook: A route for checking whether a reasoning claim survives outside the paper's favorite setting.
tracks.audience: Readers who want to evaluate reasoning models, write reproduction reports, or avoid benchmark theater.
tracks.why_hot: Hard benchmarks and robustness tests are what turn reasoning hype into something comparable.
compare: Static benchmark accuracy versus live or contamination-resistant evaluation.
Math and code reasoning versus browsing-agent reasoning.
Higher test-time compute versus robustness under perturbation.

tracks.title: Frontier Reports Context: Base Models and Reasoning Modes
tracks.hook: A route for reading model reports that contextualize the reasoning wave without being only reasoning papers.
tracks.audience: People comparing frontier model reports, base model design, synthetic data, and reasoning modes.
tracks.why_hot: Reasoning ability is now marketed and engineered as part of frontier model families, not just separate math models.
compare: Base-model architecture versus post-training recipe.
Synthetic data and math specialization versus general-purpose frontier reports.
Explicit thinking modes versus implicit reasoning improvements.
